symbols: reclining penitent figure — Mary Magdalene's withdrawal into contemplative life
skull nearby — memento mori, meditation on death and the transience of worldly beauty
angel above — divine presence and the angelic comfort sustaining the penitent
wilderness setting — the desert retreat of ascetic penitence
upward-reaching hand — aspiration toward heaven and the divine
description: An engraving showing a reclining woman with long hair and a halo in a wilderness setting, one hand reaching upward. She reclines among rocks and vegetation. Above her, a winged angel or putti descends from clouds. A skull and other objects are visible nearby. The landscape shows trees and a distant horizon. Below the image, Latin text identifies the subject as 'S. Maria Magdalene' with additional inscription. The style is classical, detailed engraving with careful crosshatching.
theological_description: This devotional print of the penitent Magdalene draws on the rich Western tradition that identified Mary Magdalene with the sinful woman who washed Christ's feet (Luke 7:37-38) and portrays her later life as a desert hermit devoted to prayer and penitence. The Latin inscription beneath — describing her love of Christ, her role as exemplar of penitence — establishes the image as a moral lesson. The Magdalene's journey from sin to sainthood embodies the core gospel promise: that no one is beyond the reach of divine grace. Her reclining posture among rocks suggests the cave of Sainte-Baume where tradition holds she spent her final years in contemplation. The angel's presence confirms that her penitence is accepted — heaven itself attends to those who truly repent. The skull she contemplates recalls the vanity of the worldly life she left behind. As the first witness of the resurrection (John 20:11-18), the Magdalene occupies a unique position in salvation history: the one from whom seven demons were cast out (Luke 8:2) became the one entrusted with the most important message in human history: 'He is risen.'
